name: Linear Programming — Best Value Under Constraints
whatIsIt: Linear programming finds the BEST value (maximum profit or minimum cost) of a linear OBJECTIVE FUNCTION, like P = 2x + 3y, when the choices are limited by linear CONSTRAINTS (inequalities). The constraints carve out a FEASIBLE REGION — the shape of all allowed (x, y). A key fact: the best value always lands at a CORNER (vertex) of that region, so you only test the corners.
whyNeeded: Businesses and planners must do the most with limited resources — maximise profit with limited materials, minimise cost while meeting demand, plan diets, transport and schedules. Linear programming is the mathematics of making the best decision under limits.
realLifeUses: Maximising factory profit with limited materials and hours
Minimising the cost of a balanced diet
Allocating a limited budget for the best return

vocabulary.meaning: The quantity to maximise or minimise, like P = 2x + 3y.
The limiting inequalities (e.g. x + y ≤ 8, x ≥ 0).
All (x, y) that satisfy every constraint — the allowed area.
A corner of the feasible region; the optimum is always at one of these.

story.title: The Toy Workshop
story.text: Isha's workshop makes x cars and y dolls, earning profit P = 2x + 3y (in hundreds). Time and wood limit her to a feasible region whose corners are (0,0), (5,0), (0,5) and (4,5). 'Where is profit biggest?' asks Papa. Isha tests each corner and finds (4,5) gives P = 2×4 + 3×5 = 8 + 15 = 23 — the highest. 'Only the corners matter!' she realises.
story.extractedProblem: Maximise P = 2x + 3y. At the corner (4, 5), what is P?

shapes.label: feasible region — test each corner for the best P

visual.caption: Constraints fence off a feasible region. The objective P = 2x + 3y hits its best value at a CORNER, so you only evaluate the corners.

teachingGallery.title: Test the corners
teachingGallery.note: Evaluate the objective at every corner; pick the biggest (max) or smallest (min).

examples.caption: The allowed choices form a shaded region with corners.
P = 2x + 3y at each corner: (4,5) wins with 23.

standardMethod.summary: Write the objective and the constraints. Draw the feasible region and find its corners. Evaluate the objective at each corner and choose the max or min.
steps: Write the objective function (what to maximise or minimise).
List the constraints as inequalities and shade the feasible region.
Find the corner points (vertices) of that region.
Put each corner into the objective function.
Pick the largest value (for a maximum) or smallest (for a minimum).

workedExamples.problem: Maximise P = 2x + 3y. Evaluate P at the corner (4, 5).
Maximise P = 5x + 4y. Evaluate P at the corner (2, 3).

commonMistakes.mistake: Looking for the best value in the MIDDLE of the feasible region.
commonMistakes.fix: For a linear objective, the best value is always at a CORNER (vertex). Test the corners, not the interior.

commonMistakes.mistake: Choosing an (x, y) that breaks a constraint.
commonMistakes.fix: Only points inside the feasible region are allowed. Check every constraint before using a point.

challenge.q: Corners give P = 10, 15 and 23. The MAXIMUM is?
Where does the best value of a linear objective occur?

options.label: At a corner (vertex) of the feasible region
In the middle of the region

challenge.answer: At a corner (vertex) of the feasible region

questions.q: Corners give P = 12, 20, 18. The maximum is?

teachBackPrompt: Give a grown-up a simple 'make x cars and y dolls' profit P = 2x + 3y with a few corner points, and show that testing corners like (4,5) → 23 finds the maximum. Explain the best value is always at a corner of the feasible region.

revisionCard.summary: Linear programming: maximise/minimise a linear objective (P = 2x + 3y) under linear constraints. The constraints make a feasible region; the optimum is always at a CORNER (vertex). Method: find corners → evaluate objective at each → pick max/min. Only points satisfying all constraints are allowed.
